summary:
The Chulmun (or Jeulmun) pottery period was the Neolithic cultural phase of the Korean Peninsula, characterized by the production of comb-pattern impressed pottery (chulmun means "comb pattern" in Korean), with origins dated to approximately 8000–7000 BCE and lasting until approximately 1500 BCE. The Chulmun ceramic tradition is broadly contemporary with the [[event:10|Jomon pottery tradition]] of Japan and the early ceramics of northeastern China, reflecting a broader East Asian pattern of early pottery production among hunter-gatherer communities.

Chulmun communities were hunter-gatherer-fishers who occupied the coastal zones, river valleys, and upland areas of the Korean Peninsula, exploiting the diverse resources of the temperate East Asian environment — deer, wild boar, and other game; fish and shellfish from rivers and coasts; and a range of plant foods including mast (acorns and other nuts). Like the Jomon peoples of Japan, Chulmun communities developed pottery technology without an agricultural foundation, using ceramics for boiling, processing, and storing food gathered rather than cultivated. The Chulmun period's long duration and wide geographic spread across the Korean Peninsula reflect the stability and productivity of hunter-gatherer lifeways in this region before the eventual adoption of rice and millet agriculture, introduced from China, around 1500–1000 BCE.
